content:
# Final Review Votes
**Producer:** agent:codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh

## Summary

Both amendments identify legitimate defects. The first is a real correctness hole in the legacy PID fallback on non-Linux Unix platforms when start time is only known to whole-second precision. The second is a straightforward platform-gating mistake in a unit test that would fail on non-Unix targets.

## Votes

- `fr-3-b97a2bb7`: Accept
  `ProcessStartTime::matches_legacy_pid_record()` currently ignores `ProcessStartPrecision` and treats any `live_started_at <= record.started_at` as a definitive match. On macOS/BSD, `process_started_at()` is rounded to whole seconds, so a reused PID that starts later in the same second can still compare as `<=` and be mislabeled `Live` even though identity is no longer proven. That is a real false-positive in the legacy path. Downgrading same-second coarse matches to `RunningUnverified` (or another non-definitive state) is the correct safety direction; if the product goal is to avoid waiting entirely, downstream handling may also need adjustment.
- `fr-3-ed328f2a`: Accept
  The test body assumes the current PID is detectable as running, but on `not(unix)` `is_pid_running_unchecked()` always returns `false`. Given this record has no authoritative identity fields and the test passes `None` for `live_started_at`, `legacy_pid_record_live_state()` will return `Stale`, not `RunningUnverified`, on non-Unix targets. Adding `#[cfg(unix)]` is consistent with the surrounding tests and fixes a real cross-platform test bug.
